%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Time Scaling

> **Section Objective:** Learn how replacing \(t\) inside \(x(t)\) compresses or expands a signal in time.

## Concepts In This Section

- Time compression
- Time expansion
- Time landmark mapping
- Piecewise signal scaling
- Sinusoid frequency change

## 1. Time Compression

Replacing \(t\) with \(nt\) inside the signal argument makes the graph happen \(n\) times faster. The same events that used to occur at large \(t\) values now occur closer to the origin.

**Symbols:** \(\phi(t)\) is the scaled signal, \(x(t)\) is the original signal, and \(n > 1\) is the compression factor.

**When to use it:** When the problem says "compressed by factor \(n\)" or shows \(x(nt)\).

**Minimal example:** If a corner of \(x(t)\) was at \(t = 6\), then in \(x(3t)\) it appears at \(t = 2\), because \(3t = 6 \Rightarrow t = 2\).

#### Exam Trigger
Inside multiplier greater than 1 → compression.

#### Common Misuse
Amplitude does **not** change. Only the time axis is affected.

$$\phi(t) = x(nt), \quad n > 1$$

## 2. Time Expansion

Replacing \(t\) with \(t/n\) inside the signal argument makes the graph happen \(n\) times slower. The same events that used to occur near the origin now move farther away from it.

**Symbol:** \(n > 1\) is the expansion factor.

**When to use it:** When the problem says "expanded by factor \(n\)", "slowed down by factor \(n\)", or shows \(x(t/n)\).

**Minimal example:** If a jump of \(x(t)\) was at \(t = -2\), then in \(x(t/4)\) it appears at \(t = -8\), because \(t/4 = -2 \Rightarrow t = -8\).

#### Exam Trigger
\(t\) is divided inside the argument → expansion.

#### Common Misuse
Students often move only positive-time landmarks correctly but forget that negative-time landmarks also move **away** from zero.

$$\phi(t) = x\!\left(\frac{t}{n}\right), \quad n > 1$$

## 3. Worked Example: Scale a Piecewise Signal

Eq. (1.6) is the original signal from Example 1.4. The key technique is to **substitute into both the condition and the formula** — do not just change the exponent.

**Compression by 3:** Let \(x_c(t) = x(3t)\).
- Condition \(-1.5 \le t < 0\) becomes \(-1.5 \le 3t < 0\), so \(-0.5 \le t < 0\).
- Condition \(0 \le t < 3\) becomes \(0 \le 3t < 3\), so \(0 \le t < 1\).
- Formula \(2e^{-t/2}\) becomes \(2e^{-3t/2}\).

**Expansion by 2:** Let \(x_e(t) = x(t/2)\).
- The support \([-1.5,\, 3)\) stretches to \([-3,\, 6)\).
- Formula \(2e^{-t/2}\) becomes \(2e^{-t/4}\).

#### Exam Note
Always solve the inequalities after substitution. Do not guess the new interval from the old one by inspection.

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 2, & -1.5 \le t < 0 \\ 2e^{-t/2}, & 0 \le t < 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \tag{1.6}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.7](/figures/page-075-unknown-1.png)
*Figure 1.7 shows the original signal \(x(t)\), its compressed version \(x_c(t) = x(3t)\), and its expanded version \(x_e(t) = x(t/2)\) — notice that the amplitude level 2 is identical in all three plots while the time landmarks shift.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">Three stacked time-domain plots share a horizontal \(t\)-axis and a vertical amplitude axis. In the top plot, \(x(t)\) equals 2 from \(t = -1.5\) to \(t = 0\) and then follows a decaying exponential \(2e^{-t/2}\) until \(t = 3\). In the middle plot, \(x_c(t) = x(3t)\) keeps the same peak amplitude of 2 but all time landmarks move inward: the flat segment runs from \(-0.5\) to \(0\) and the exponential \(2e^{-3t/2}\) decays faster, ending at \(t = 1\). In the bottom plot, \(x_e(t) = x(t/2)\) keeps the same amplitude but stretches the support outward: the flat segment runs from \(-3\) to \(0\) and the slower exponential \(2e^{-t/4}\) ends at \(t = 6\). The figure makes explicit that time scaling moves endpoints and changes decay rate without altering vertical amplitude.</div>

## 4. Sinusoids Under Time Scaling

Compressing a sinusoid by factor \(n\) multiplies its angular frequency by \(n\). Amplitude \(A\) and phase \(\theta\) are completely unaffected.

**Example:** \(\sin(2t)\) compressed by 3 becomes \(\sin(2 \cdot 3t) = \sin(6t)\). The signal completes cycles three times faster.

**Expansion counterpart:** Expanding by factor \(n\) divides the frequency by \(n\), so the signal completes cycles more slowly.

#### Exam Trigger
If the factor is **inside** the sine argument multiplying \(t\), it changes frequency — not amplitude.

#### Common Misuse
Saying compression makes the peaks taller. It does not. Only the horizontal time axis is affected.

$$A\sin(\omega t + \theta) \;\ \longrightarrow \;\ A\sin(n\omega t + \theta), \quad n > 1$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Compression:** \(\phi(t) = x(nt),\ n > 1\) — inside factor greater than 1 pulls the graph inward.
- **Expansion:** \(\phi(t) = x(t/n),\ n > 1\) — dividing \(t\) inside pushes the graph outward.
- **Landmark rule:** for \(x(at)\), each time landmark moves to \(t_{\text{new}} = t_{\text{old}} / a\); amplitude is unchanged.
- **Sinusoid rule:** \(A\sin(\omega t + \theta) \to A\sin(a\omega t + \theta)\) — only frequency scales; \(A\) and \(\theta\) stay fixed.

*Next, time reversal will show what happens when the time axis is reflected.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
